q.en: When and where was Thurgood Marshall born?
q.zh: 瑟古德·马歇尔(Thurgood Marshall)是哪一年、在哪里出生的？

options.en: In 1908, in Baltimore, Maryland
options.zh: 1908年，生于马里兰州巴尔的摩

options.en: In 1908, in Topeka, Kansas
options.zh: 1908年，生于堪萨斯州托皮卡

options.en: In 1933, in Washington, D.C.
options.zh: 1933年，生于华盛顿特区

options.en: In 1967, in Baltimore, Maryland
options.zh: 1967年，生于马里兰州巴尔的摩

explain.en: The book says, 'Thurgood Marshall was born in 1908 in Baltimore, Maryland.'
explain.zh: 书里写道：『瑟古德·马歇尔 1908 年出生于马里兰州巴尔的摩。』

q.en: What was Thurgood Marshall's original first name, and why did he change it?
q.zh: 瑟古德·马歇尔原本的名字叫什么？他为什么改了？

options.en: He was originally 'Thoroughgood' but shortened it to Thurgood in second grade because he didn't like spelling it
options.zh: 他原本叫『Thoroughgood』，二年级时嫌难拼，改成了 Thurgood

options.en: He was originally 'Theodore' and changed it to honor his father
options.zh: 他原本叫 Theodore，为纪念父亲而改

options.en: He was born 'Thurgood' and added 'Marshall' later
options.zh: 他出生时叫 Thurgood，后来才加上 Marshall

options.en: He was originally 'Goody' and took 'Thurgood' from a book
options.zh: 他原本叫 Goody，从一本书里取了 Thurgood

explain.en: A 'Do You Know?' box says he was originally named 'Thoroughgood' but 'Because he didn't like having to spell it, he shortened his name to Thurgood in second grade.' His nickname was 'Goody.'
explain.zh: 书中「你知道吗？」写道，他原名『Thoroughgood』，『因为不喜欢拼写这个名字，二年级时他把名字改短成 Thurgood』。他的小名是『Goody』。

q.en: What happened when Marshall tried to attend the University of Maryland Law School?
q.zh: 马歇尔想进马里兰大学法学院时，发生了什么？

options.en: He was denied admission because of his race, so he went to Howard University and graduated top of his class in 1933
options.zh: 他因种族原因被拒，于是去了霍华德大学，1933 年以全班第一毕业

options.en: He was admitted and became the school's star student
options.zh: 他被录取了，并成为学校的明星学生

options.en: He decided not to study law at all
options.zh: 他决定干脆不学法律了

options.en: He was accepted only after his father paid a large fee
options.zh: 在父亲交了一大笔费用后才被录取

explain.en: The book says 'Because of his race, the school would not let him in. Instead, he went to the all-black law school at Howard University. He graduated at the top of his class in 1933.'
explain.zh: 书里说：『因为他的种族，学校不让他入学。于是他去了全黑的霍华德大学法学院。1933 年他以全班第一毕业。』

q.en: In the book, what did the Supreme Court mean by saying segregated places were 'separate but equal'?
q.zh: 在书中，最高法院说被隔离的场所『separate but equal』（隔离但平等）是什么意思？

options.en: It declared segregation legal, claiming restaurants, trains, schools, and hotels for black and white people were allowed to be separate as long as they were 'equal'
options.zh: 它宣布种族隔离合法，声称只要「平等」，黑人和白人的餐厅、火车、学校、旅馆可以分开

options.en: It said all schools must immediately mix black and white students
options.zh: 它说所有学校必须立刻让黑白学生同校

options.en: It meant black and white people could never be separated
options.zh: 意思是黑人和白人永远不能被分开

options.en: It was a rule that only applied to water fountains
options.zh: 这是一条只适用于饮水机的规则

explain.en: The book says the Supreme Court 'had made segregation legal. It decided that those restaurants, trains, schools, parks, and hotels were 'separate but equal.'
explain.zh: 书里说最高法院『让种族隔离合法化。它裁定那些餐厅、火车、学校、公园和旅馆是「隔离但平等」的。』

q.en: According to the glossary, what is 'discrimination'?
q.zh: 根据词汇表，『discrimination』（歧视）是什么意思？

options.en: The unfair treatment of a person or group based on differences such as race, gender, age, or religion
options.zh: 基于种族、性别、年龄、宗教等差异，对一个人或一群人不公平的对待

options.en: The legal right to vote in elections
options.zh: 在选举中合法的投票权

options.en: A law passed by the Supreme Court
options.zh: 最高法院通过的一条法律

options.en: The act of going to school
options.zh: 上学这件事

explain.en: The glossary defines discrimination as 'the unfair treatment of a person or group based on gender, race, age, religion, or other differences.'
explain.zh: 词汇表把 discrimination 定义为『基于性别、种族、年龄、宗教或其他差异，对一个人或一群人不公平的对待』。

q.en: In Brown v. Board of Education, what unfair situation did Marshall point out about schools?
q.zh: 在「布朗诉教育局案」(Brown v. Board of Education) 中，马歇尔指出的学校不公平现象是什么？

options.en: Black children had to attend all-black schools even when a white school was much closer, and got less money; he argued 'separate was not equal'
options.zh: 黑人孩子即使离白人学校近得多，也得去全黑学校，而且经费更少；他认为『隔离并不平等』

options.en: White children were not allowed to attend school at all
options.zh: 白人孩子根本不被允许上学

options.en: All children went to the same schools but the buildings were too crowded
options.zh: 所有孩子都上同一所学校，只是校舍太拥挤

options.en: Black children got more money for school than white children
options.zh: 黑人孩子得到的教育经费比白人孩子多

explain.en: The book says many states 'spent far less money on black students than on white students' and black children 'had to attend all-black schools, even when they lived miles closer to an all-white school.' Marshall argued 'separate was not equal.'
explain.zh: 书里说很多州『花在黑人学生身上的钱远少于白人学生』，黑人孩子『即使住得离全白学校近几英里，也得去全黑学校』。马歇尔主张『隔离并不平等』。

q.en: What was the result of Marshall's victory in Brown v. Board of Education?
q.zh: 马歇尔在「布朗诉教育局案」中胜诉，带来了什么结果？

options.en: Every Supreme Court justice voted to end school segregation, helping end other segregation and inspiring the civil rights movement
options.zh: 最高法院全体大法官投票结束学校隔离，进而推动了其他隔离的废除，并点燃了民权运动

options.en: The Court said segregation should continue in schools
options.zh: 最高法院说学校隔离应当继续

options.en: Only the state of Kansas changed its laws
options.zh: 只有堪萨斯州修改了法律

options.en: Marshall lost the case but became famous anyway
options.zh: 马歇尔虽然输了官司，却因此出名

explain.en: The book says 'Every justice on the Supreme Court voted to end school segregation. That decision helped end other kinds of segregation... It inspired the civil rights movement.'
explain.zh: 书里说『最高法院每一位大法官都投票结束学校隔离。这一判决也推动了其他形式的隔离被废除……并点燃了民权运动。』

q.en: According to the book, why did Thurgood Marshall decide to become a lawyer?
q.zh: 根据这本书，瑟古德·马歇尔为什么决定成为一名律师？

options.en: He realized that by becoming a lawyer he could use the law to help African Americans achieve equality
options.zh: 他意识到，成为律师就能用法律帮助非裔美国人争取平等

options.en: His father forced him to study law
options.zh: 他父亲逼他学法律

options.en: He wanted to earn a lot of money
options.zh: 他想赚很多钱

options.en: He lost a case and wanted to defend himself
options.zh: 他打输了一场官司，想亲自为自己辩护

explain.en: The book says he felt segregation 'went against the ideas and laws put forth in the Constitution' and 'He realized that by becoming a lawyer, he could use the law to help African Americans achieve equality.'
explain.zh: 书里说，他认为隔离『违背了宪法提出的理念和法律』，并『意识到，成为律师就能用法律帮助非裔美国人争取平等』。

q.en: What important 'first' did Thurgood Marshall achieve in 1967?
q.zh: 1967 年，瑟古德·马歇尔达成了哪一项重要的「第一」？

options.en: He became the first African American to serve as a justice on the U.S. Supreme Court, chosen by President Lyndon Johnson
options.zh: 他成为美国最高法院首位非裔大法官，由林登·约翰逊总统提名

options.en: He became the first African American President of the United States
options.zh: 他成为美国首位非裔总统

options.en: He was the first African American to graduate from law school
options.zh: 他是首位从法学院毕业的非裔美国人

options.en: He won his first case before the Supreme Court
options.zh: 他在最高法院赢了第一场官司

explain.en: The book says 'In 1967, President Lyndon Johnson chose Marshall to be a justice on the Supreme Court. He became the first African American to ever serve on the highest court of the United States.'
explain.zh: 书里说：『1967 年，林登·约翰逊总统提名马歇尔为最高法院大法官。他成为美国最高法院历史上首位非裔大法官。』

q.en: When asked if black people were better off, what did Marshall say, and what was his bigger belief?
q.zh: 当被问到黑人的处境是否改善时，马歇尔怎么回答？他更大的信念是什么？

options.en: He said 'All Americans are better off' and believed the law was the root of equality and the only thing that brings permanent change
options.zh: 他说『所有美国人的处境都更好了』，并相信法律是平等的根基、也是带来永久改变的唯一力量

options.en: He said only black Americans improved and the law was useless
options.zh: 他说只有非裔美国人变好了，法律毫无用处

options.en: He said nothing had changed since he joined the Court
options.zh: 他说自他上任以来什么都没改变

options.en: He believed money, not law, was the key to equality
options.zh: 他相信金钱而非法律才是平等的关键

explain.en: The book quotes him: 'All Americans are better off since I joined the Court... All of them.' It also says he 'believed that it was the root of equality... and that only the law would bring permanent change.'
explain.zh: 书里引用他的话：『自我上任以来，所有美国人的处境都更好了……所有人。』书还说，他『相信法律是平等的根基……只有法律才能带来永久的改变』。

prompt.en: Thurgood Marshall used the law—not violence—to fight unfair rules, and he won 29 of 32 cases before the Supreme Court. Think of a rule or situation at your school or in your community that you think is unfair. What could people do, following Marshall's example, to try to change it peacefully? Use evidence from the book about how Marshall worked.
prompt.zh: 瑟古德·马歇尔用法律而不是暴力来对抗不公的规则，在最高法院赢了 32 场中的 29 场。想一想你学校或社区里一条你认为不公平的规矩或现象。效仿马歇尔，人们可以怎样和平地改变它？用书中关于马歇尔如何努力的史实来支持你的想法。

en: Marshall believed the Constitution's rights should apply to everyone and used the law to win equality (p. 7).
He argued cases like Brown v. Board of Education and won when every justice voted to end school segregation (p. 11).
A good answer names a real unfair rule and suggests a peaceful, lawful way to change it, like Marshall did.
zh: 马歇尔相信宪法赋予的权利应人人平等，并用法律争取平等（第7页）。
他打了像「布朗诉教育局案」这样的官司，并在全体大法官投票结束学校隔离时胜诉（第11页）。
好的回答要说出一条真实的不公平规矩，并提出像马歇尔那样和平、合法的改进办法。
